body.from: Comments now trigger, and should log this change in comment body.

comment.body:
refactor(audit): simplify LAST_100 columns and use comment-specific links
    
    Streamline LAST_100 readability and link precision.
    
    - remove Details column from LAST_100 table
    - render Issue column as linked issue number only
    - render Milestone column as linked milestone number only
    - prefer comment.html_url for target links (issuecomment anchors)
      with fallback to issue/milestone URLs
    
    Detailed before/after content remains available via per-event artifact links in the Event ID column.

body.from: Hash organized diffs should now be available.

comment.body:
fix(audit): prefix every edited comment line in diff block
    
    Update comment body diff rendering to apply +/- prefixes to each line for multiline edits.
    
    - replace single printf lines with line-wise sed prefixing
    - preserve fenced diff block format for GitHub red/green rendering
    
    This ensures multiline comment edits are fully represented in per-event markdown diffs.

body.from:
refactor(audit): simplify LAST_100 columns and use comment-specific links
    
    Streamline LAST_100 readability and link precision.
    
    - remove Details column from LAST_100 table
    - render Issue column as linked issue number only
    - render Milestone column as linked milestone number only
    - prefer comment.html_url for target links (issuecomment anchors)
      with fallback to issue/milestone URLs
    
    Detailed before/after content remains available via per-event artifact links in the Event ID column.

milestone.title: NonPriority
milestone.description: Just a placeholder for issues that should be fixed, but not currently within an existing milestone.

milestone.title: PermissionHandlingUpdatesTEST
milestone.description:
# Milestone Summary
Initializing  publishers blocks the main thread  leading to an unresponsive application.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Objective

Use **Kotlin Coroutines** to eliminate main thread blocking and enable
smooth publisher initialization.

# Publisher Initialization Blocking UI

## Current State

After the device connects, the app calls:

`onSerialReady()`

Inside this method:

`publisherManager.initializePublishers()`
`publisherManager.startPublishers()`

During initialization, the app:

-   Requests several permissions
-   Waits for them to be granted

This process blocks the **UI completely**, causing the app to become
unresponsive.

## Expected State

In `AbcvlibActivity`:

-   Move the `onSerialReady` callback execution context to the **Default
    Dispatcher**

This will:

-   Allow publishers to initialize asynchronously
-   Handle permission requests without blocking the UI

TEST

# Milestone Summary
Initializing  publishers blocks the main thread  leading to an unresponsive application.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Objective

Use **Kotlin Coroutines** to eliminate main thread blocking and enable
smooth publisher initialization.

# Publisher Initialization Blocking UI

## Current State

After the device connects, the app calls:

`onSerialReady()`

Inside this method:

`publisherManager.initializePublishers()`
`publisherManager.startPublishers()`

During initialization, the app:

-   Requests several permissions
-   Waits for them to be granted

This process blocks the **UI completely**, causing the app to become
unresponsive.

## Expected State

In `AbcvlibActivity`:

-   Move the `onSerialReady` callback execution context to the **Default
    Dispatcher**

This will:

-   Allow publishers to initialize asynchronously
-   Handle permission requests without blocking the UI

In the absence of the custom robot hardware, it would be useful to have an instrumentation test that mimics the communication between the two. In this way both remote and automated tests could be performed at runtime.

https://github.com/tekkura/smartphone-robot-android/blob/3f31a3b4769493a56f6d145aeb8aaa41c6ca0cdb/libs/abcvlib/src/main/java/jp/oist/abcvlib/util/SerialCommManager.java#L110

That is the function that would have to be called by the test, with command = SET_MOTOR_LEVELS as thats really the only one being used right now. You'd have to create a test definition for an instance of https://github.com/tekkura/smartphone-robot-android/blob/main/libs/abcvlib/src/main/java/jp/oist/abcvlib/util/RP2040State.java.

## USB-Serial scope
Issues under this milestone are also related to the USB-serial communication protocol.

The main focus is:
1. Refactor low-level handling of start/end flags and other enveloping in favor of robust high-level read/write abstractions with intuitive defaults for common USB-serial errors/exceptions.
2. Reduce communication loop time between phone and firmware. Current round-trip latency is approximately 20 ms; identify contributors via targeted profiling and optimize accordingly.

### Background
Another scope is currently focused on refactoring the firmware of the external USB connected robot to use USB-CDC via TinyUSB instead of the current stdio-based USB-serial implementation. Many issues encountered during this project appear to stem from instability in the low-level USB-serial layer. By migrating to TinyUSB on RP2040, firmware aims to improve stability, separation of concerns, and long-term maintainability.

This work is dependent on progress in the firmware repo, so implementation that depends on firmware protocol changes should wait until firmware-side readiness is established.

TODO:
Add details about the expected communication to mimic.

comment.body: No, this is not the way to bring the changes from fork

issue.title: Evaluate Python Android framework candidates with explicit selection criteria

comment.body:
> I don't think of it as a good idea. Now with the advent of claude code or openai codex, developing on platforms/languages of which a user doesn't have much knowledge, has become easy. The research users potentially would be fast to develop natively via claude code than to learn the python framework for android.

A fair argument 😅 but we have this scope approved and funded, so we need to finish these points at least. Implementing it in great depth is outside the scope, just an evaluation on existing options. Most researchers go through mandatory coding courses at OIST and python is the most used language for that, thus the motivation to provide something with similar syntax. I still think its a good idea though as even if you depend on LLMs, its still good to know how to at least read your own code.

issue.title: Evaluate Python Android framework candidates with explicit selection criteria

comment.body: Also another argument is that these researchers need to develop their own novel algorithms for their research. These are things LLMs don't know anything about, so they can't help. They can of course depend on them for syntax fixing and explaining code, etc, but for generating their research ideas it wouldn't work as the research isn't published anywhere for an LLM to know about.

with the guide contents. 

Also another thing I'd like to confirm here. The dependency on the `io.github.nishkarsh:android-permissions` was to be refactored out in favor of modern native methods to Android. 

It is still in:

  - Dependency wired in module:
      - libs/abcvlib/build.gradle.kts:48 → api(libs.android.permissions)
  - Version catalog alias:
      - gradle/libs.versions.toml:77 → io.github.nishkarsh:android-permissions
  - Actual API usage in code:
      - libs/abcvlib/src/main/java/jp/oist/abcvlib/core/inputs/Publisher.java:11
      - libs/abcvlib/src/main/java/jp/oist/abcvlib/core/inputs/Publisher.java:54
      - libs/abcvlib/src/main/java/jp/oist/abcvlib/core/inputs/phone/MicrophoneData.java:14

Does this fit easily into this milestone for you? This was one of the original main goals of it.

comment.body:
@topherbuckley 
- The original plan to refactor permissions handling was mainly to fix the UI freezing caused by the Java-based implementation. 
- Now initializing publishers inside a coroutine makes the freezing issue is resolved.

- The library ` io.github.nishkarsh:android-permissions` still functioning well. We can either 
**keep it as is**, or 
**refactor it to use modern native Android permission APIs**

But that would extend the scope of this milestone.

issue.title: AbcvlibController– Some Properties Violate the Java2Kotlin Migration Guide
AbcvlibController– Some Properties Violate the Java2Kotlin Migration Guide

body.from:
### Summary

### Summary

Both `_isRunning` and `_output` in [`AbcvlibController`](https://github.com/tekkura/sr-android/pull/163/changes#diff-3ca45e1bf592ec5582592836a84968c58fc6e67fc92139baba37e6b48fd21d16) do not follow the [Java2Kotlin Migration Guide](https://github.com/tekkura/sr-android/blob/milestone/java2kotlin/docs/migrations/Java2Kotlin.md).

> Do not bypass JVM accessor clashes by renaming canonical properties to underscore-backed names (for example, `state` → `_state`) when the rename is only used to avoid getter/setter conflicts.

### Current state

```kotlin
private var _isRunning = false
private var _output: Output = Output()
```

### Expected state

```kotlin
private var isRunning = false
private var output: Output = Output()
```

@Ma7moud3ly Done. I rebased both milestone/PermissionHandlingUpdates onto main then PermissionHandlingUpdates/Migration-Guide onto milestone/PermissionHandlingUpdates

@topherbuckley thanks
waiting for your comments or approval
@Ma7moud3ly I have a few questions on Issue #14 before I can review this with a good enough understanding. See my comment here: https://github.com/tekkura/sr-android/issues/14#issuecomment-3980117890

@topherbuckley 
> Rather than just wrapping the `onSerialReady` contents inside a `lifecycleScope.launch` call? i.e.

### This will not work as expected ❌
- We would need to apply this change across all 9 apps
- `onSerialReady` in `AbcvlibActivity` is already overridden and is invoked from `MainActivity`.

For example, in `BasicQrreceiver`:

```kotlin
override fun onSerialReady(usbSerial: UsbSerial) {
    publisherManager = PublisherManager()

    val qrCodeData = QRCodeData.Builder(this, publisherManager, this).build()
    qrCodeData.addSubscriber(this)

    publisherManager.initializePublishers()
    publisherManager.startPublishers()

    setSerialCommManager(SerialCommManager(usbSerial))
    super.onSerialReady(usbSerial)
}
```
-  All of the `MainActivity.onSerialReady` logic would remain at the main thread.
- Only `super.onSerialReady(usbSerial)` content and `onOutputsReady` will be shifted
- The apps will still unresponsive

@topherbuckley 
> What happened to the apparent need to "Move permission handling to the Activity." and "Request permissions before initializing the USB connection."? That you outlined in the issue description? I'm not seeing this mentioned in the kotlin version of the fix, or the migration guide you proposed in https://github.com/tekkura/sr-android/pull/169/changes. Can you clarify if this is no longer needed or should be also included in the migration guide?

- I have explained before that we don't need to modify anything related to the permissions handling.. Kotlin coroutines can make everything work as expected with minor changes.   https://github.com/tekkura/sr-android/issues/14#issuecomment-3966306015
- While the millstone refers to update permissions handling, the actual changes are related to fix the main thread blocking made by requesting permissions at `Publisher.kt`

@topherbuckley 
> Rather than just wrapping the `onSerialReady` contents inside a `lifecycleScope.launch` call? i.e.

### This will not work as expected ❌
- We would need to apply this change across all 9 apps
- `onSerialReady` in `AbcvlibActivity` is already overridden and is invoked from `MainActivity`.

For example, in `BasicQrreceiver`:

```kotlin
override fun onSerialReady(usbSerial: UsbSerial) {
    publisherManager = PublisherManager()

    val qrCodeData = QRCodeData.Builder(this, publisherManager, this).build()
    qrCodeData.addSubscriber(this)

    publisherManager.initializePublishers()
    publisherManager.startPublishers()

    setSerialCommManager(SerialCommManager(usbSerial))
    super.onSerialReady(usbSerial)
}
```
-  All of the `MainActivity.onSerialReady` logic would remain at the main thread.
- Only `super.onSerialReady(usbSerial)` content and `onOutputsReady` will be shifted
- The apps will still unresponsive 
- The proposed solution aims to centralize one fix to all apps
- It will not use lambada
The expected changed:
 ```kotlin
            this.usbSerial = UsbSerial(
                context = this,
                usbManager = usbManager,
                serialReadyListener = object : SerialReadyListener {
                    override fun onSerialReady(usbSerial: UsbSerial) {
                        lifecycleScope.launch(Dispatchers.Default) {
                            this@AbcvlibActivity.onSerialReady(usbSerial)
                        }
                    }
                }
            )
```

body.from:
@topherbuckley 
> Rather than just wrapping the `onSerialReady` contents inside a `lifecycleScope.launch` call? i.e.

### This will not work as expected ❌
- We would need to apply this change across all 9 apps
- `onSerialReady` in `AbcvlibActivity` is already overridden and is invoked from `MainActivity`.

For example, in `BasicQrreceiver`:

```kotlin
override fun onSerialReady(usbSerial: UsbSerial) {
    publisherManager = PublisherManager()

    val qrCodeData = QRCodeData.Builder(this, publisherManager, this).build()
    qrCodeData.addSubscriber(this)

    publisherManager.initializePublishers()
    publisherManager.startPublishers()

    setSerialCommManager(SerialCommManager(usbSerial))
    super.onSerialReady(usbSerial)
}
```
-  All of the `MainActivity.onSerialReady` logic would remain at the main thread.
- Only `super.onSerialReady(usbSerial)` content and `onOutputsReady` will be shifted
- The apps will still unresponsive

comment.body:
> @topherbuckley I don't think using gradle.properties is a good idea since this file is version-controlled and thus publicly available.

Agreed! Looks good to me.
#codex-review

@GeorgeChernyshov @topherbuckley

As a part of phase 1, I reviewed the `ObjectDetector` Subscriber to understand the expected changes. I noticed that the class exposes `onObjectsDetected` callback to three apps: `handsOnApp`, `basicSubscriber`, and `basicCharger`.

```kotlin
override fun onObjectsDetected(
    bitmap: Bitmap,
    tensorImage: TensorImage,
    results: MutableList<Detection>,
    inferenceTime: Long,
    height: Int,
    width: Int
)
```

Tensorflow dependencies 

- `tensorImage` is currently not used anywhere.
- `List<Detection>` or the new mediapipe `List<ObjectDetectorResult>` could be mapped to a **custom data class**. This would fully decouple the apps from the internal object detector implementation.
```
CustomDetection #0:
  Box: (x: , y: , w: , h: )
  Categories:
    index      :
    score      :
    class name :
```

```kotlin
override fun onObjectsDetected(
    bitmap: Bitmap,
    results: List<CustomDetection>,
    inferenceTime: Long,
    height: Int,
    width: Int
)
```

Benefits:
- Clear separation of concerns between the detector and apps.
- Easier to maintain, extend, and refactor.
- The library user does not need to implement TensorFlow or MediaPipe directly.
Rebased the branch onto main
Rebased both branch and PR onto `milestone/AppSigning`
@topherbuckley please recheck to see if branch is correct now
#codex-review
Rebased onto milestone/TensorFlowLite2LiteRT
#codex-review

@topherbuckley what is your opinion on @Ma7moud3ly's idea? I think it is good both for clean architecture and for user experience. However, it would require us to decide what data CustomDetection contains. I don't have enough understanding of the project to decide on it myself (I might, but I'll probably make a wrong judgement)

> @GeorgeChernyshov @topherbuckley
> 
> As a part of phase 1, I reviewed the `ObjectDetector` Subscriber to understand the expected changes. I noticed that the class exposes `onObjectsDetected` callback to three apps: `handsOnApp`, `basicSubscriber`, and `basicCharger`.
> 
> ```kotlin
> override fun onObjectsDetected(
>     bitmap: Bitmap,
>     tensorImage: TensorImage,
>     results: MutableList<Detection>,
>     inferenceTime: Long,
>     height: Int,
>     width: Int
> )
> ```
> 
> Tensorflow dependencies
> 
>     * `tensorImage` is currently not used anywhere.
> 
>     * `List<Detection>` or the new mediapipe `List<ObjectDetectorResult>` could be mapped to a **custom data class**. This would fully decouple the apps from the internal object detector implementation.
> 
> 
> ```
> CustomDetection #0:
>   Box: (x: , y: , w: , h: )
>   Categories:
>     index      :
>     score      :
>     class name :
> ```
> 
> ```kotlin
> override fun onObjectsDetected(
>     bitmap: Bitmap,
>     results: List<CustomDetection>,
>     inferenceTime: Long,
>     height: Int,
>     width: Int
> )
> ```
> 
> Benefits:
> 
>     * Clear separation of concerns between the detector and apps.
> 
>     * Easier to maintain, extend, and refactor.
> 
>     * The library user does not need to implement TensorFlow or MediaPipe directly.

I don't like this idea. I think we should maintain a dependency on MediaPipe directly. Reasoning:
1. We'd have to add+maintain documentation on custom interface. MediaPipe documentation is already well established. Lets make use of that rather than try to replace/wrap it. 
2. Updates to mediapipe/liteRT could break the custom interface unknowingly. It creates maintenance work. 
3. Our users will be familiar with Tensorflow and related libraries, so changing the interface to something custom is undesirable

issue.title: Evaluate Python Android framework candidates with explicit selection criteria

comment.body:
@topherbuckley

This milestone looks interesting to me, but I'm not completely sure I understand the  expected results.

From the description, it seems that the goal might be:
-   To provide a list of Python frameworks which can be used to create Android applications
-   To include two demo apps , as referenced in #154

However, I'm not fully certain if in this milestone scope intended to use `abcvlib` from a Python script🤔

Mainly there are several frameworks at  the official Python doc https://wiki.python.org/moin/Android.html. I have used `Kivy` and `Chaquopy` which  is probably the most straightforward option for integrating Python into an Android app and interacting with Java/Kotlin libraries such as `abcvlib`.

#### Example of using `abcvlib` from a Python script hosted on an Android app using Chaquopy plugin.

### `MainActivity.kt`

``` kotlin
Python.start(AndroidPlatform(this))
val py = Python.getInstance()
val module = py.getModule("main")
module.callAttr("init_usb", this)
```

### `app/src/main/python/main.py`

``` python
from jp.oist.abcvlib.util import UsbSerial, SerialReadyListener
from android.content import Context
from java import dynamic_proxy


class SerialListener(dynamic_proxy(SerialReadyListener)):

    def onSerialReady(self, usbSerial):
        print("USB ready from Python")
        print(usbSerial)


def init_usb(activity):
    usbManager = activity.getSystemService(Context.USB_SERVICE)
    listener = SerialListener()

    try:
        UsbSerial(activity, usbManager, listener)
        print("UsbSerial created:", usbManager)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Error creating UsbSerial:", e)
```

issue.title: Evaluate Python Android framework candidates with explicit selection criteria

comment.body:
@Ma7moud3ly thanks for the detailed inquiry! 

>However, I'm not fully certain if in this milestone scope intended to use abcvlib from a Python script🤔

It doesn't necessarily have to expose everything from abcvlib, but if, for example, it can implement the same things the `basicSubscriber` or `basicAssembler` apps do, that would be enough. Essentially there needs to be a way in python to read the inputs and control the outputs of the system:

Inputs = Data from any of the publisher classes
Outputs = setWheelOutput, generate QR Code

Your example code above would be too low-level for the needs. Python shouldn't  need to touch anything related to the serial connection code itself. All of that init code should remain on the Android side and run prior to any python code starting. You might think of it like sandboxing the python code to the abcvlibMainLoop. 

Let me know if that makes any sense and if I can expand on anything to make the goal any clearer.

comment.body:
> So, you are thinking about something like `Arduino`?
> 
> void setup() {
>     // Initialization code here
> }
> 
> void loop() {
>     // Main loop code here
> }

Yeah, I think that would work. 

> You don't need basic users to think about `abcvlib` interop like this:
> 
> from jp.oist.abcvlib.core.inputs.microcontroller import WheelData
> 
> Instead, we provide a simpler library module:
> 
> from abcvlib import PublisherManager, WheelData
> publisherManager = PublisherManager()
> # Build WheelData instance using the PublisherManager
> wheelData = WheelData.Builder(publisherManager)
> ....

I don't think this would be necessary. This would imply a fair bit of wrapping and documentation, which I'd like to avoid. I think it would be easier to maintain/document if we still refer to the original Kotlin library  if users want to understand the underlying code for whatever reason. In other words, I don't think squashing everything into a single `abcvlib` import is necessary. Keeping it like `from jp.oist.abcvlib.core.inputs.microcontroller import WheelData` would be better both from a maintainability and clearness to the users where the underlying code resides.

> I suppose there is a reason to use runnables instead of coroutines.

@GeorgeChernyshov can you expand on this comment? We are implementing runnables instead of coroutines and you think we should do the opposite or what? I'm not familiar enough with the kotlin terminology to know exactly what defines a coroutine, so I need some help understanding this.

issue.title: Evaluate Python Android framework candidates with explicit selection criteria

comment.body:
@topherbuckley 

# Python-on-Android Framework Comparison

## Summary
Here, we review two Python-on-Android toolchains for milestone demonstrations and evaluate whether they can be used with abcvlib.
---

## 1. Framework Comparison

| Criterion | Chaquopy | Kivy / python-for-android + jnius |
|---|---|---|
| **Android Studio / Gradle** | Native Gradle plugin / zero extra toolchain | Requires separate build via `buildozer` or `p4a` CLI |
| **Setup complexity** | Low / add plugin + DSL block in `build.gradle` | High / Python build environment, NDK config, recipes |
| **Native Android API access** | Full, via standard Java/Kotlin reflection (`from com.example import ...`) | Via `jnius` (PyJNIus) / reflection-based, more boilerplate |
| **Call Python from Java/Kotlin** | Yes / first-class `Python.getInstance()` API | Indirect / subprocess or JNI bridge only |
| **AAR / local library integration** | Standard Gradle `implementation` of `.aar` | Not supported natively / no Gradle integration |
| **Packaging / APK output** | Standard Gradle `build`  | `buildozer android release` / separate artifact |
| **UI layer** | Android native (XML layouts, Jetpack Compose, etc.) | Kivy OpenGL canvas / custom widget set, not native |
| **License** | Free for open source; commercial license for paid apps | MIT / Apache 2 |
| **Best for this repo (abcvlib)** | ✅ Strongly preferred / Gradle-native, AAR-compatible |  Viable fallback, more integration friction |

---

## 2. Accessing Native Android APIs

### 2a. Chaquopy / Direct Java/Kotlin Import
Chaquopy exposes the full Android classpath to Python via standard import syntax. Any class available to the Gradle build is importable.

---

### 2b. Kivy / jnius / `autoclass` Reflection
jnius wraps Android classes via `autoclass`. Every method call goes through JNI reflection.
---

## 3. Code Example / `PublisherManager` + `WheelData`

### Original Kotlin

```kotlin
val publisherManager = PublisherManager()
val wheelData = WheelData.Builder(this, publisherManager)
    .setBufferLength(50)
    .setExpWeight(0.01)
    .build()
```

---

### 3a. Chaquopy Translation

```python
# src/main/python/robot_setup.py

from jp.oist.abcvlib.core import PublisherManager
from jp.oist.abcvlib.data import WheelData

def setup_wheel_data(activity):
    publisher_manager = PublisherManager()

    wheel_data = (
        WheelData.Builder(activity, publisher_manager)
        .setBufferLength(50)
        .setExpWeight(0.01)
        .build()
    )

```

Kotlin host side:

```kotlin
// In AbcvlibActivity.onCreate() or onSerialReady()
val py = Python.getInstance()
val module = py.getModule("robot_setup")
```

- `this` (the `Activity`) is passed directly / no conversion needed.
- Package path `jp.oist.abcvlib.data.WheelData` must match exactly what is in the `.aar`.

---

### 3b. Kivy / jnius Translation

```python
# main.py (Kivy app)

from jnius import autoclass

PublisherManager = autoclass('jp.oist.abcvlib.core.PublisherManager')
WheelData = autoclass('jp.oist.abcvlib.data.WheelData')
PythonActivity = autoclass('org.kivy.android.PythonActivity')

def setup_wheel_data():
    activity = PythonActivity.mActivity

    publisher_manager = PublisherManager()

    wheel_data = (
        WheelData.Builder(activity, publisher_manager)
        .setBufferLength(50)
        .setExpWeight(0.01)
        .build()
    )
```

- `autoclass` performs reflection at runtime
---

## 4. Limitations

### Chaquopy Limitations

| Area | Limitation |
|---|---|
| **License** | Free only for open-source apps. Paid apps require a commercial license |
| **APK size** | Embeds the CPython interpreter (+8–15 MB compressed). |
| **No Compose-from-Python** | UI must be written in Kotlin/Java/XML. Python is logic/scripting only. |

---

### Kivy / python-for-android + jnius Limitations

| Area | Limitation |
|---|---|
| **Build complexity** | Requires Linux build environment, NDK, buildozer configuration / CI setup is non-trivial. |
| **No Gradle integration** | Cannot consume `.aar` files natively / requires manual extraction and classpath hacks. |
| **jnius reflection overhead** | Every Java method call crosses the JNI boundary via reflection |
| **Runtime errors only** | Class/method name typos in `autoclass` are not caught until runtime. |
| **UI is not native** | Kivy renders its own OpenGL canvas |

---

## 5. Shortlist & Recommendation

| Rank | Framework | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **1st / Chaquopy** | Full Gradle integration means `abcvlib.aar` works with zero friction. Python imports Java classes directly.. no reflection boilerplate. Clean interop with existing Kotlin|
| **2nd / Kivy + jnius** | Higher integration cost, especially for `.aar` consumption and USB serial callbacks. |

---

Reference: [Python on Android Wiki](https://wiki.python.org/moin/Android.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

comment.body:
@Ma7moud3ly  thank you for the outline and examples. A few questions:

## Q1
> Are you ok with this Structure?
> 
> ```
> backandforthPython
> │
> ├── src/main/java/.../MainActivity.kt
> │
> └── backandforthPython/src/main//python
>     ├── abcvlib.py
>     └── main.py
> ```

Is that the standard structure for Chaquopy? The replication of the kotlin/Android structure I can understand, but I'm not sure I understand the reasoning for replicating the same structure inside `backandforthPython/backandforthPython`. It can't just be:

> ```
> backandforthPython
> │
> ├── src/main/java/.../MainActivity.kt
> │
> └──python/
>     ├── abcvlib.py
>     └── main.py
>```
? Its not a huge deal either way, just want it as easily understandable as possible without causing you too much extra work. 

---------------------
## Q2
in MainActivity
>`setupModule.put("loop_delay", 0.005) // 5 ms`

overrides this in python/abcvlib.py? 

>`loop_delay = 0.010`

Or the opposite? In the end, which file has the final say in the value used?

-----------
## Q3
Will this `loop_delay` set the executor fixed delay from [abcvlibMainLoop](https://github.com/tekkura/sr-android/blob/dd26648fc1d7a065413ce715956c424aa99a4023/libs/abcvlib/src/main/java/jp/oist/abcvlib/core/AbcvlibActivity.kt#L76-L78)
? Or are these independently created loops?

issue.title: Build equivalent demo apps in shortlisted Python Android frameworks

comment.body:
>Q3 ? Or are these independently created loops?

- It is not recommended to use `abcvlibMainLoop` from Kotlin to repeatedly call a loop function inside Python, because there is a delay in the Java–Python interface. Therefore, I preferred to implement the loop function directly inside Python.
- The `loop_delay` is injected from Kotlin to match the same delay used by `abcvlibMainLoop`.

comment.body:
> > Q2 overrides this in python/abcvlib.py?
> 
> Yes, the value actual is injected from kotlin `loop_delay = 0.010` just a placeholder

Ok, so you set a default in kotlin and it can be overriden in the python files yes? The python file should have the final say if there are multiple definitions (i.e. if the user overrides this value, it should actually be overriden not remain the default or remain a different value set in the kotlin code). 

> * It is not recommended to use `abcvlibMainLoop` from Kotlin to repeatedly call a loop function inside Python, because there is a delay in the Java–Python interface. Therefore, I preferred to implement the loop function directly inside Python.
> 
> * The `loop_delay` is injected from Kotlin to match the same delay used by `abcvlibMainLoop`.

I see. Yes, I think that should be fine.

I see. Can you refer me somewhere that I can identify how to generate these appropriately and what scope they would need as secrets? I can populate them thereafter.

@topherbuckley 
RELEASE_KEYSTORE_BASE64 - BASE64-encoded version of your keystore
RELEASE_CERTIFICATE_SHA256 - SHA256 of your keystore
RELEASE_STORE_PASSWORD - Keystore password
RELEASE_KEY_ALIAS - key name
RELEASE_KEY_PASSWORD - key password

The changes here are just to fix the build test failure on your PR. (With side effects of potentially speeding up the build test workflow as well). We don't need to test signing release builds with every PR, we're just looking to catch build/compile errors in the code. 

Let me know if I've misunderstood something though.

Because this was a java project 🤔

Add a script to upload apks in a github release

comment.body:
Follow-up from reproducing and isolating the failure on the `oist/smartphone-robot-android` side.

The core issue turned out to be narrower than a general workflow permission problem.

What was failing:
- The GitHub Release / APK upload path was not the blocker.
- The failing step was Gradle package publication: `:abcvlib:publishDebugPublicationToGitHubPackagesRepository`.
- The workflow run already showed `Packages: write`, so this was not simply a missing `GITHUB_TOKEN` scope in `automated-release.yml`.

How it was determined:
- I normalized the published version from `vX.Y.Z` to `X.Y.Z`; the publish still failed with `422 Unprocessable Entity`.
- I then changed only the Maven artifact id from `abcvlib` to a temporary `abcvlib-test`; that publish succeeded.
- That isolated the failure to the existing canonical package identity `jp.oist:abcvlib`, not to the workflow, token, or Gradle publish mechanism in general.
- Inspecting GitHub Packages showed that the old private `jp.oist.abcvlib` package was still associated with the archived `oist/smartphone-robot-archive` repository.

Fix:
- Delete the obsolete private `jp.oist.abcvlib` package that was still attached to the archived repo.
- Restore the canonical artifact id `abcvlib` in the active repo.
- Publish again from `oist/smartphone-robot-android`, which recreates the package under the correct repository lineage.

Relevance to this issue:
- If we keep the current release workflow and add scripting around it, GitHub Release asset upload and Maven package publication should be treated as separate concerns.
- The release upload path was already working; the AAR publish failure came from stale GitHub Packages ownership/state for `jp.oist:abcvlib`, not from the release-upload logic itself.

Investigation update relevant to Maven publication:

We reproduced the GitHub Packages publish failure from the automated release workflow and traced it to package identity state, not workflow auth.

What was determined:
- The failing workflow run already had `GITHUB_TOKEN` permissions including `Packages: write`, so this was not a missing-permissions / token-scope issue.
- The original failure was a `422 Unprocessable Entity` on `:abcvlib:publishDebugPublicationToGitHubPackagesRepository` while uploading the AAR.
- We normalized the published Maven version to remove the leading `v` from tags (for example `v2.0.0` -> `2.0.0`) and retried with a fresh tag. The publish still failed with the same `422`, which ruled out the version-prefix as the root cause.
- We then changed only the publication artifact ID from `abcvlib` to a temporary `abcvlib-test` and published again with a fresh tag. That publish succeeded.

Root cause:
- The specific package identity `jp.oist:abcvlib` was the problem.
- The old private `jp.oist.abcvlib` package was still associated with the archived `oist/smartphone-robot-archive` repository rather than the active `oist/smartphone-robot-android` repository.
- Because of that stale GitHub Packages state, publishing the canonical package name from the active repo was rejected.

How it was fixed:
- The obsolete private `jp.oist.abcvlib` package attached to the archived repo was deleted.
- The publication artifact ID was restored back to `abcvlib`.
- Publishing from the active repo can now recreate the canonical package under the correct repository/package lineage.

Why this matters for Maven Central work:
- The failure mode was not a Gradle publishing bug and not a workflow-token bug; it was namespace / package-ownership state on the target registry.
- When moving to Maven Central, we should treat namespace ownership and package lineage explicitly so old registry state does not cause the same kind of block again.

comment.body:
Closing this in favor of the APKDistribution route chosen in 2026.

We are not treating Maven Central as the current implementation target for this milestone. The selected public-consumption path is JitPack for `abcvlib`, tracked in:
- #196 Make `abcvlib` buildable on JitPack without GitHub credentials
- #195 Document APK releases on GitHub and public `abcvlib` consumption via JitPack

The implementation PR for that route is #199.
